intent: Check whether the candidate understands NestJS structure as composition boundaries, routing, and injectable behavior.
primaryQuestion: What are Module, Controller, Service, and Provider in NestJS, and how do they relate?

requirements.description: Module groups related providers and controllers.
Imports and exports define dependency visibility.
Controller handles incoming HTTP route mapping.
Controller should delegate business work rather than contain all logic.
Service is a common injectable provider containing business or application logic.
Provider is any injectable class/value/factory managed by Nest IoC container.
Module wires controller and providers together.
Controller injects service/provider through constructor to handle request.

scoringHints.description: Covers the mechanism, practical example, edge cases, and production consequence with clear reasoning.

Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

primaryQuestion: What is Dependency Injection in NestJS, how does the IoC container work, and what is the difference between @Injectable() and @Inject()?

requirements.description: Dependencies are provided from the outside instead of manually constructed inside consumers.
Nest container resolves providers and injects them where needed.
Marks a class as participating in Nest provider metadata and DI system.
The class must still be registered in a module providers array or exported/imported path.
Specifies an injection token explicitly.
Useful for interfaces, strings/symbols, custom providers, or when type metadata is insufficient.
Providers can be mocked or overridden in TestingModule.
DI supports swapping infrastructure implementations.

intent: Check whether the candidate can place cross-cutting concerns in the right lifecycle layer.
primaryQuestion: What is the NestJS request lifecycle order: Middleware, Guards, Interceptors, Pipes, Route Handler, Interceptors, Exception Filters?

expectedSignals.label: Lists lifecycle order at a useful level.

requirements.description: Middleware runs early before guards.
Guards decide whether route handler can execute.
Interceptors wrap handler execution.
Pipes transform/validate route arguments before handler.
Filters handle thrown exceptions.

expectedSignals.label: Explains inbound versus outbound interceptor behavior.

requirements.description: Interceptor can run logic before handler.
Interceptor can transform or observe result/error after handler.
DTO validation and transformation belong in pipes such as ValidationPipe.
Pipes operate on parameters before route handler receives them.
Auth guard before route handler.
Logging or response mapping in interceptor.
Centralized error formatting in filter.

intent: Assess whether the candidate maps authentication and authorization to route-aware lifecycle components.
primaryQuestion: What is a Guard in NestJS, how is it different from Middleware, and when would you use a Guard versus Middleware for authentication?

requirements.description: Middleware runs before route handling and can modify request/response or call next.
Middleware is useful for generic request preprocessing.
Guard implements canActivate and decides if current request may proceed.
Guard has ExecutionContext and can read route/class metadata.
Authentication and authorization that depend on route metadata fit guards well.
Roles or permissions decorators are naturally read in guards.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions middleware possible but limited for auth.

requirements.description: Middleware can parse token or attach raw context.
It is less ideal for route-specific authorization decisions.

whitelist strips properties without decorators.

transform converts payloads to DTO instances or primitive types when configured.

Whitelisting reduces mass assignment and unexpected-field bugs.

expectedSignals.label: Defines interceptor as wrapping route handler execution.

requirements.description: Interceptor can run logic before handler.
Interceptor can transform response stream or handle errors after handler.

expectedSignals.label: Gives logging and timing use case.

requirements.description: Measures duration and logs request/response metadata safely.
Avoids logging secrets.

expectedSignals.label: Gives response transformation or caching use case.

requirements.description: Can wrap response shape consistently.
Can integrate caching behavior around handler result.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes from guard, pipe, and filter.

requirements.description: Does not use interceptor as primary authorization decision point.
Does not use interceptor as DTO validation replacement.
Exception filters are better for centralized error formatting.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands Nest module boundaries and avoids assuming every provider is globally visible.
primaryQuestion: What does @Module({ imports, controllers, providers, exports }) mean, and how do imports and exports control provider visibility?

requirements.description: imports brings in other modules.
controllers declares route handlers for the module.
providers declares injectable dependencies owned by the module.
exports exposes selected providers/modules to importing modules.
Providers are visible inside their declaring module.
Other modules can inject them only if exported by an imported module.
OrdersModule needs UsersService from UsersModule.
UsersModule must export UsersService and OrdersModule must import UsersModule.
Export only public capabilities, not every internal helper.
Avoid turning modules global just to skip explicit imports.

intent: Check whether the candidate can wire abstractions, configuration, and test doubles through Nest provider definitions.
primaryQuestion: What is a custom provider in NestJS? Compare useClass, useValue, useFactory, and useExisting.

requirements.description: Custom provider explicitly maps an injection token to a provider strategy.
Useful when class type alone is not enough.
useClass tells Nest which class to instantiate for the token.
useValue supplies an existing object, constant, mock, or config value.
useFactory computes provider value, often using injected dependencies or async config.
useExisting aliases one token to another existing provider.

expectedSignals.label: Connects tokens to interfaces and tests.

requirements.description: Interfaces disappear at runtime, so token is needed for interface-like injection.
Tests can override providers with mocks.

Explains the main idea correctly but misses one nuance such as a boundary case, trade-off, or failure mode.
Shows surface familiarity but cannot apply the concept to a realistic code or API scenario.
Answer is too vague or memorized to validate practical understanding.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands configurable module metadata and feature-level provider registration.
primaryQuestion: What is a dynamic module, and why do modules like ConfigModule.forRoot() or TypeOrmModule.forFeature() use this pattern?

requirements.description: A module can return module metadata programmatically.
It can configure imports, providers, and exports based on options.
forRoot usually configures global or application-level module settings.
Examples include loading configuration, connection options, or clients.
forFeature registers feature-specific providers such as repositories for selected entities.
It lets feature modules import only what they need.
Keeps configuration centralized while feature dependencies remain explicit.
Avoids manually wiring repetitive providers in every module.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can separate transport shape, persistence mapping, and business rules.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between DTOs, entities, and domain models in a NestJS backend?

requirements.description: DTO describes data crossing API boundaries such as request body or response shape.
DTOs often carry validation decorators.
Entity often maps to persistence table or ORM model.
It may include database columns and relations.
Domain model represents business concepts and invariants.
It should not be forced to mirror database or HTTP shape exactly.
Avoids exposing persistence fields in public API.
Keeps validation, persistence, and business rules from becoming tangled.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can handle circular dependencies pragmatically while recognizing bad module boundaries.
primaryQuestion: What is a circular dependency in NestJS, how can forwardRef() help, and when is it a sign to refactor?

requirements.description: Module or provider A depends on B while B also depends on A.
It can happen at module import or provider injection level.
forwardRef delays resolving the reference so Nest can handle some cycles.
It is a workaround, not always a design fix.
Frequent circular dependencies suggest unclear ownership or bidirectional feature coupling.
Shared orchestration may belong in a third service/module.
Extract shared interface, domain service, event, or mediator/orchestrator.
Keep one feature from depending on another feature internal implementation.
